content.text: In the context of language models,

means anchoring the model's output in verifiable external information rather than in the model's internal parametric memory. A grounded answer is one where every factual claim can be traced back to a specific source document that was provided to the model at inference time.

RAG achieves grounding by injecting retrieved documents directly into the prompt before generation. When the relevant information is present in the context window, the transformer's attention mechanism weights those tokens far more strongly than the diffuse prior knowledge encoded in the model's weights — effectively reading from the context rather than recalling from memory.

attrs.alt: Parametric memory (frozen weights) vs in-context knowledge (retrieved documents): parametric knowledge is compressed and can hallucinate; in-context knowledge is exact and attributable. When they conflict, in-context wins. Grounding techniques shown at the bottom: prompt instruction, citation format, low temperature, faithfulness check.

content.text: Language models have two sources of knowledge during inference:

information compressed into the model's weights during training. It covers the general knowledge present in the training corpus but is frozen at the training cutoff. The model cannot update it without retraining.

information present in the current prompt. The model can read tokens in the context window with full attention. This knowledge is ephemeral — it exists only for the duration of this inference call — but it is exact and can be arbitrarily recent.

for instruction-tuned models. The attention pattern shows that tokens in the context window receive much higher attention weights than the diffuse influence of the model's prior beliefs. This is the mechanism that makes RAG effective.

and provides relevant passages will almost always produce an answer that reflects the context, not the model's prior. This is true even when the context contradicts the model's training — for example, a context saying the capital of a fictional country is 'Zephyria' will cause the model to answer accordingly, overriding any parametric belief.
The transformer's scaled dot-product self-attention computes:

At each layer, every token in the output attends to every token in the context. When the context contains the answer to the query, the attention heads that specialise in factual recall strongly activate on the relevant context tokens — their

products are large, producing high softmax weights, which cause the output representation to be dominated by those context values. The parametric priors (encoded in the feed-forward layers) contribute additively but are outweighed when context signal is strong.
This is why grounding quality improves with:
if the relevant passage is absent from the context, the model falls back to parametric memory. Putting the right text in the prompt is the single biggest lever for grounding quality.
instruction-tuned models respond strongly to phrases like
. These instructions activate the model's instruction-following capability, further suppressing parametric recall.
in many models, context placed immediately before the question receives higher attention weight than context placed far earlier in the prompt (the
effect). Placing the most relevant passage last, just before the question, tends to improve grounding.
The most common form: the model answers by reading retrieved documents. Every claim in the response can be verified against a specific passage.

# Document-grounded prompt template
def build_grounded_prompt(query: str, docs: list[dict]) -> str:
    context_parts = []
    for i, doc in enumerate(docs, 1):
        source = doc.get("metadata", {}).get("source", f"doc-{i}")
        context_parts.append(f"[{i}] {doc['page_content']}\n(Source: {source})")

    context = "\n\n".join(context_parts)
    return (
        "You are a precise assistant. Answer the question using ONLY "
        "the provided context. If the context does not contain the answer, "
        "say 'Not found in the provided documents.'\n\n"
        f"Context:\n{context}\n\n"
        f"Question: {query}\n"
        "Answer (cite the source number for each claim, e.g. [1], [2]):"
    )

When specific entities (people, companies, products) are mentioned in the question, the retriever should surface documents that contain those exact entities. Embedding-based retrieval can conflate similar entities; hybrid BM25+dense retrieval or filtered search by entity field improves entity grounding.
For time-sensitive queries, ground the answer in documents with a known date. Store a
field in the document metadata and pass it to the LLM alongside the content:

def build_temporal_prompt(query: str, docs: list[dict]) -> str:
    context_parts = []
    for i, doc in enumerate(docs, 1):
        meta = doc.get("metadata", {})
        date_str = meta.get("date", "unknown date")
        source = meta.get("source", f"doc-{i}")
        context_parts.append(
            f"[{i}] (Date: {date_str}, Source: {source})\n{doc['page_content']}"
        )
    context = "\n\n".join(context_parts)
    return (
        "Answer the question using only the context. "
        "If multiple dates exist, prefer the most recent information.\n\n"
        f"Context:\n{context}\n\n"
        f"Question: {query}\n"
        "Answer (include the date of the information):"
    )

Research by Liu et al. (2023) showed that language models perform best when the relevant information appears at the beginning or end of a long context window — performance degrades for information placed in the middle. This is the
put the highest-relevance passages first and last. Middle positions lose attention weight.
rather than injecting all top-K passages, inject only the top 3–5 most relevant ones. A smaller, tightly relevant context outperforms a large, noisy one in grounding quality.
a cross-encoder reranker can select the
relevant passage from a larger candidate set, giving you the best single passage to ground the answer.

def reorder_for_grounding(docs: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    """Place highest-ranked doc last (just before question) for better attention."""
    if len(docs) <= 1:
        return docs
    # docs assumed sorted by relevance (best first)
    # Strategy: best goes last, second-best goes first, rest in between
    best = docs[0]
    rest = docs[1:]
    return rest + [best]
Grounding Failures and How to Detect Them
RAG does not guarantee grounded output. The following failure modes can cause the model to generate ungrounded claims even with retrieved context:
Long context; relevant passage in middle
Natural Language Inference (NLI) models can check whether each claim in the answer is entailed by the retrieved context — a technique used by the RAGAS faithfulness metric:

from transformers import pipeline

# Small cross-encoder trained on NLI (entailment/neutral/contradiction)
_nli = pipeline("text-classification", model="cross-encoder/nli-deberta-v3-small")

def split_into_sentences(text: str) -> list[str]:
    """Stub — use spaCy or NLTK in production."""
    return [s.strip() for s in text.replace("?", ".").split(".") if s.strip()]

def faithfulness_score(answer: str, context: str) -> float:
    """
    For each sentence in the answer, check if context entails it.
    Returns fraction of sentences that are entailed.
    """
    sentences = split_into_sentences(answer)
    if not sentences:
        return 1.0

    entailed = 0
    for sent in sentences:
        result = _nli(f"{context} [SEP] {sent}")[0]
        if result["label"] == "entailment":
            entailed += 1

    score = entailed / len(sentences)
    print(f"Faithfulness: {score:.2f} ({entailed}/{len(sentences)} sentences entailed)")
    return score

# Example
context = "Acme Corp Q3 2025 revenue was $2.1B, up 8% YoY."
answer  = "Acme Corp reported Q3 2025 revenue of $2.1B with 8% growth. The CEO expects Q4 to be stronger."

faithfulness_score(answer, context)
# Faithfulness: 0.50  (1/2 sentences entailed)
# The second sentence about Q4 expectations is not grounded in the context.
The single most effective technique for improving grounding is the system prompt instruction. Instruction-tuned models are sensitive to explicit constraints.

GROUNDING_SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a precise, fact-checking assistant.

Rules:
1. Answer ONLY using information from the provided Context.
2. If the Context does not contain enough information to answer, respond:
   "The provided documents do not contain this information."
3. Cite the source for every factual claim using [N] notation.
4. Do not use any knowledge from your training data to supplement the Context.
5. Do not speculate about information not in the Context."""

def build_grounded_messages(query: str, docs: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    context = "\n\n".join(
        f"[{i}] {doc['page_content']} (source: {doc['metadata'].get('source', '?')})"
        for i, doc in enumerate(docs, 1)
    )
    return [
        {"role": "system", "content": GROUNDING_SYSTEM_PROMPT},
        {"role": "user",   "content": f"Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {query}"},
    ]

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=build_grounded_messages(query="What was Q3 revenue?", docs=retrieved_docs),
    temperature=0,     # deterministic → less deviation from context
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
Requiring the model to cite a

reference for every claim is one of the strongest grounding techniques. It forces the model to identify the source passage before making the claim — acting as a self-imposed grounding check. If the model cannot find a citation, it is more likely to say

CITATION_PROMPT = """Answer the question below. For each factual statement,
cite the document it comes from using [N] where N is the document number.
If you cannot find support in the documents, write [NOT FOUND].

Example format:
"The revenue was $2.1B [1]. Growth was driven by cloud services [2]."
"""

Modern LLMs (Gemini 1.5 Pro, Claude, GPT-4o) support context windows of 128K–1M tokens, raising the question: can we simply put the entire knowledge base in the context and skip RAG? The answer for most production use cases is no, for four reasons:
Even at 128K tokens, grounding quality degrades for passages in the middle of the context. Retrieval lets you select the
passages and place them near the question.
Filling a 128K context with tokens costs far more than retrieving 3 relevant passages and filling 2K tokens. For high-traffic applications, the cost difference is 50–100×.
Prefill time for 128K tokens adds 2–5 seconds of latency. RAG with 2K context adds < 50 ms.
If the knowledge base changes daily, a cached 128K context is stale. RAG fetches the current state of the index on every query.
Long context is most useful as a
to RAG — retrieve a short list of candidate passages, then pass them all to a long-context model for synthesis. This is sometimes called RAG + summarisation or
Fraction of answer claims entailed by context
% of answers with at least one ungrounded claim
% of cited passages that actually support the claim

import asyncio
from openai import AsyncOpenAI
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

client = AsyncOpenAI()
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
store = Chroma(embedding_function=embeddings, collection_name="kb")

SYSTEM = (
    "Answer using ONLY the numbered documents below. "
    "Cite [N] for every claim. If not found, say so."
)

async def grounded_rag(query: str, k: int = 4) -> dict:
    # Retrieve
    docs = store.similarity_search(query, k=k)

    # Build grounding prompt
    context = "\n\n".join(
        f"[{i}] {d.page_content} — {d.metadata.get('source','?')}"
        for i, d in enumerate(docs, 1)
    )
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM},
        {"role": "user",   "content": f"Documents:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {query}"},
    ]

    # Generate
    resp = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=messages,
        temperature=0,
        stream=False,
    )
    answer = resp.choices[0].message.content

    # Faithfulness check (lightweight — one claim at a time)
    faith = faithfulness_score(answer, context)

    return {
        "answer": answer,
        "sources": [d.metadata.get("source") for d in docs],
        "faithfulness": faith,
    }

result = asyncio.run(grounded_rag("What was Q3 2025 revenue?"))
print(result["answer"])
print("Faithfulness:", result["faithfulness"])
print("Sources:", result["sources"])
Not including a grounding instruction in the system prompt.
The default instruction-following behaviour still allows the model to supplement context with parametric knowledge. Explicit
instructions are necessary for high-stakes grounding requirements.
More context is not always better. 10 loosely-relevant passages will dilute the signal from the 2 highly-relevant ones and increase the lost-in-the-middle effect. Retrieve more, inject fewer.
A faithful answer is grounded in the retrieved text — but the retrieved text might itself be wrong or outdated. Faithfulness measures document-adherence, not truth.
but passage 3 does not actually support the claim, the citation is a hallucination that passed faithfulness checks. Spot-check a random sample of citations.
A retrieved passage about a company's revenue from 2022 can produce a grounded-but-stale answer for a 2025 query. Store and expose dates in document metadata; include them in the prompt.
Use a strict grounding system prompt
with explicit instructions to answer only from context and to cite sources. Test the prompt by inserting a context that contradicts a well-known fact — the model should follow the context.
Measure faithfulness before measuring answer quality.
If faithfulness is below 0.8, fix the retrieval and prompt before tuning for answer completeness.
Place the most relevant passage closest to the question
in the prompt to counter the lost-in-the-middle effect. A reranker helps select and order passages.
Store temporal and provenance metadata with every document
at index time. A passage without a date or source is harder to ground correctly.
Higher temperatures introduce stochasticity that can cause the model to deviate from context. Use higher temperatures only when creative elaboration is desired alongside factual grounding.
Add a faithfulness gate in production
for high-stakes use cases (legal, medical). If faithfulness drops below a threshold, escalate to human review rather than returning a potentially ungrounded answer.
Grounding means every factual claim is traceable to a retrieved source document.
This is what separates a RAG answer from a hallucinated one.
when the prompt is designed correctly: in-context information strong-attends and overrides the model's prior beliefs.
means relevant passages placed deep in a long context receive less attention — keep contexts short and reorder passages so the most relevant are nearest the question.
Faithfulness (NLI-based entailment checking) is the primary grounding metric.
Target > 0.85; below 0.8 indicates the model is adding information not in the context.
Prompt engineering — strict grounding instruction + citation format + temperature=0
— is the most effective lever for improving grounding without changing the model.
Long context is a complement, not a replacement, for RAG
: retrieval selects relevant passages; long context then synthesises them more thoroughly.
